one_line_takeaway: Approximate machine unlearning cannot be trusted to scrub data-poisoning effects: across 8 methods and 4 attack types it never matches retrain-from-scratch, standard MIAs mask the failure, and the authors' cheap one-run Gaussian-poison audit reveals the surviving influence.
key_finding: No single unlearning algorithm mitigates all poisoning types; none matches retraining-from-scratch on both post-unlearning test accuracy and poison removal. Standard MIAs (Shokri et al.) give low TPR@FPR=0.01 and wrongly suggest unlearning succeeded, whereas the Gaussian-poison audit exposes surviving poison influence at the same low FPR. Success is both attack-dependent and task-dependent: e.g. GD removes indiscriminate poison in vision but fails on targeted/Gaussian; SCRUB partly handles Gaussian poison in text but fails on targeted/indiscriminate; GA helps for Resnet-18 but not GPT-2; EUk/NGD handle Gaussian on GPT-2 with a classification head yet fail on the text-decoder targeted attack. Root causes: poison samples induce a much larger ℓ1 model shift than removing random clean samples (Hypothesis 1), and that shift lies largely in a subspace orthogonal to clean-sample gradient directions (low cosine similarity), so clean-data gradient updates cannot counteract it within budget (Hypothesis 2).
core_contribution: The paper (ICLR 2025, Pawelczyk et al.) empirically demonstrates that current approximate machine-unlearning methods for deep learning FAIL to remove the effects of data poisoning, even when the unlearner is handed the complete set of poison samples as the forget set and given a generous compute budget. Its own contributions are: (1) a large-scale stress test of eight state-of-the-art unlearning algorithms against indiscriminate, targeted, backdoor, and a newly introduced Gaussian poisoning attack, showing none consistently matches retraining-from-scratch; (2) the novel Gaussian data poisoning attack/metric, which adds visually-undetectable i.i.d. Gaussian noise to a small fraction of training inputs and later measures the normalized inner product Iz = <gz, ξz>/(εp||gz||2) between the stored noise and the model's input-gradient — this doubles as a computationally cheap membership-inference/audit that needs only ONE training run rather than the hundreds of shadow models prior MIA-based unlearning audits require; (3) two validated hypotheses for WHY unlearning fails (large model shift; shift lies in a subspace orthogonal to clean-sample gradients).
threat_model: Two intertwined threat models. (i) Data-poisoning adversary: modifies a small fraction bp<<1 of training samples (x,y)->(x+Δ(x),y) to induce indiscriminate accuracy degradation, targeted/backdoor misclassification of specific inputs, or hidden Gaussian signals that leak into model gradients. (ii) Unlearning as a defense/remediation: a model owner, after discovering poisoning (or receiving a deletion request), runs an approximate unlearning algorithm on the identified poison set U=Spoison hoping to restore the clean model without full retraining. The paper's central security claim is that this remediation is unreliable — approximate unlearning leaves residual poison effects, giving a false sense of confidence, and it also implies an auditor's perspective (the Gaussian metric lets an auditor detect that unlearning did not truly occur at low FPR).
limitations: Scope is modest-scale: two datasets (CIFAR-10, IMDb) and two model families (Resnet-18, GPT-2 355M) — no billion-parameter LLMs despite framing about industrial-scale systems. The mechanistic hypotheses are validated only on convex linear/logistic regression over frozen Resnet-18 features, not on the deep nonconvex models themselves. The setup assumes the defender KNOWS and is handed the exact full poison set (the easiest case for unlearning); real defenders must first identify poisons. Compute budget of 10% is a chosen threshold; results depend on hyperparameter/learning-rate search (linear search 1e-6 to 5e-5). NGP and GA can collapse to random-guess accuracy, indicating brittle tuning rather than a fair capability comparison. The Gaussian attack is a diagnostic/audit construct rather than a naturally occurring threat.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (50,000 training images, 10 classes) — vision task, Resnet-18
IMDb sentiment reviews (25,000 training samples, binary polarity) — language task, GPT-2 355M
quantitative_results: 8 unlearning methods evaluated; none matches retrain-from-scratch across all poison types
Poison budget primarily 1.5% of training data (also swept 2% and 2.5%)
Compute budget capped at 10% of original training (=10 epochs CIFAR-10 / 1 epoch IMDb); also swept 4/6/8%
Indiscriminate-poisoned Resnet-18 test accuracy: 81.67% / 77.20% / 69.62% for 750 / 1000 / 1250 poison points

Table 1 (indiscriminate, CIFAR-10, 10-epoch unlearn budget): retrain baseline reaches 87.04% (2 epochs) up to 89.24% (10 epochs); GD unlearning of 1.5% poison reaches 83.67% (2 ep) to 87.71% (10 ep); EUk collapses to ~25-50%; NGP and GA degenerate to 10% (random guess) at all poison budgets
SCRUB stays ~83-85% test accuracy on the indiscriminate task but does not fully close the gap to retraining
MIA audit measured at TPR@FPR=0.01; standard MIA yields low TPR (near-trivial), Gaussian-poison MIA yields higher TPR at the same FPR, revealing residual poison
Applying NGD to the GPT-2 LLM dropped post-unlearning test accuracy by ~10%
Results averaged over 8 runs (vision) / 5 runs (language), ±1 std
GPT-2 vocabulary C=50,257; fine-tune LR 5e-5, 10 epochs
baselines_compared: SSD (Selective Synaptic Dampening, Foster et al.)
Standard membership-inference attack (Shokri et al. 2017) as the prior unlearning-evaluation baseline the paper argues is misleading
